one_line_takeaway: Mining reusable natural-language 'security specifications' from historical patches and CVEs and feeding them as retrieval-augmented reasoning context lifts LLM vulnerability detection to 45.0% F1 / 37.7% recall on PrimeVul under strict reasoning-aware (CORRECT) evaluation—a ~33% relative F1 gain—though absolute accuracy stays low and per-case cost is heavy.
key_finding: On the PrimeVul pairwise test split (838 samples, 50% vulnerable) with DeepSeek-V3, VulInstruct reaches 45.0% F1 (vs 33.9% for the strongest baseline GPTLens, +32.7% relative) and 37.7% recall (+50.8% relative), while keeping 55.8% precision; it uniquely detects 24.3% of the 226 CVEs found across all systems (2.4x more than any baseline). In pairwise discrimination it hits 17.2% P-C (+32.3% relative) and 7.4% VP-S. Ablations show both specification types matter and, crucially, that DYNAMIC abstraction is essential: feeding raw retrieved CVE descriptions instead of abstracted domain-specific specs drops F1 from 45.0% to 36.5%. Removing domain-specific specs drops F1 to 40.4% and collapses VP-S from 7.4% to 1.0%; removing general specs drops F1 to 41.0%. Gains transfer across the independent SVEN benchmark (57.3% F1 vs GPTLens 34.1%) and across base models (e.g., Claude-Sonnet-4 VP-S 2.2%->7.3%; DeepSeek-R1 becomes the strongest configuration at 22.8% P-C).
core_contribution: VulInstruct is a specification-guided, retrieval-augmented approach for LLM-based vulnerability detection. Its central claim is that state-of-the-art LLMs fail at vulnerability detection because they lack 'security specifications'—the implicit developer/security-team expectations about how code must behave to stay safe—which are rarely explicit in training data. VulInstruct systematically mines these expectations from historical vulnerabilities and injects them as natural-language reasoning context. Two complementary specification types are built by two automatic pipelines: (i) GENERAL specifications, pre-extracted offline from high-quality vulnerable/patched patch pairs (formatted 'HS-[DOMAIN]-[ID]: [expected safe behavior]', each grounded in a structured case containing a threat model, vulnerability analysis, and solution analysis), and (ii) DOMAIN-SPECIFIC specifications ('AS-[DOMAIN]-[ID]'), dynamically abstracted at detection time from CVE cases retrieved from an NVD-derived domain evidence base. The distinguishing design choice versus prior work (Vul-RAG, formal-rule miners like APHP/Seal) is expressing specifications as reusable natural language rather than concrete code examples or structural patterns over dependence graphs, which the authors argue is easier to acquire (just prompt an LLM on patches) and generalizes across projects. NOTE: much of Section 2 surveys prior systems (PrimeVul, VulLLM, R2Vul, ReVD, GPTLens, VulTrial, Savant, BugScope); those are cited context, not this paper's contribution.
threat_model: This is a DEFENSIVE tooling paper: the artifact is a detector, not an attack. The vulnerabilities it targets are conventional software security flaws in C/C++ source—memory-safety and resource-management bugs (CWE-787 out-of-bounds write, CWE-125 out-of-bounds read, CWE-476 NULL deref, CWE-416 use-after-free), improper input validation (CWE-20), info exposure (CWE-200), etc. The implicit adversary is an attacker who supplies crafted inputs (e.g., malformed image headers with untrusted size fields causing integer/buffer overflow, or a malicious TLS server exploiting missing hostname verification) to exploit code that violates an unstated safe-behavior expectation. The paper's 'threat model' term refers to a per-specification artifact: a system-level description of trust boundaries, attack surfaces, and the vulnerability chain attached to each stored case to ground the rule in a realistic attack scenario. Practical validation: the tool discovered a previously unknown high-severity logic vulnerability in production code, a variant of a known CVE violating the same specification, later assigned CVE-2025-56538 (responsibly disclosed, confirmed and patched by developers).
limitations: Absolute performance remains modest—best F1 is 45.0% and P-C only 17.2%—so this is an incremental gain over a weak state of the art, not a solved problem. The dominant failure mode (from manual analysis: 33 added true positives but 11 added false positives when comparing DeepSeek-R1 +/- VulInstruct) is over-flagging: the model correctly spots the vulnerable version but fails to recognize that a highly domain-specific patch (e.g., LJPEG decoder logic) actually fixed it, a capability boundary of current LLMs. Computational cost is high: 150.6s and ~18.4K input tokens per case sequentially (dominated by domain-evidence retrieval 45.3s and knowledge scoring 55.3s), reducible to 106.9s with an inverted index and parallel scoring. Evaluation depends on an LLM-as-Judge (GPT-5) with only 82% author agreement (below the 92% reported for GPT-4o in original CORRECT), acknowledged as a potential bias. The domain-specific ranking uses hand-tuned heuristics (attacker-term presence, length, recency, >500-CVE keyword cutoff). The authors also excluded PrimeVul's training set (lacked CVE/CWE mappings and context) and note NVD references (PoCs, patch lists, crash reports) are under-exploited.

datasets_used: PrimeVul (evaluation benchmark; official pairwise test split, 838 samples, 50% vulnerable / 50% patched; temporal train/test split)
CORRECT (knowledge source for general specifications; 2000 vulnerable-patched function pairs with callee/type/global/import context; 1,338 pairs used, all before PrimeVul test cutoff)
NVD/CVE corpus (domain evidence base; all valid CVEs published 2002-2024, yielding 173,070 valid and 15,391 rejected cases)
SVEN (cross-benchmark generalization; all C/C++ cases = 417 vulnerable functions with vulnerable-patch pairs)
quantitative_results: PrimeVul (DeepSeek-V3): VulInstruct F1 45.0%, recall 37.7%, precision 55.8%, accuracy 53.9%, Unique 24.3%, P-C 17.2%, VP-S 7.4%
Relative improvement over best baseline: F1 +32.7%, recall +50.8%, P-C +32.3%, Unique +138.2% (recall gain also stated as +50.8% vs GPTLens)
226 total CVEs detected across all systems; VulInstruct uniquely detects 24.3%, 2.4x more than any baseline
State-of-the-art LLMs achieve <12% accuracy distinguishing vulnerable from patched code (PrimeVul finding, motivation)
Ablation: w/o domain-specific specs F1 40.4% (VP-S 1.0%); w/o general specs F1 41.0% (VP-S 3.7%); w/o dynamic spec generation (raw CVE descriptions) F1 36.5%
Knowledge-utilization subsets: General+Domain-specific present on 73.5% of samples -> 48.3% F1; general-only 25.7% -> 42.5% F1; no knowledge 0.7% -> 0% F1
Threshold sweep (100-pair subset): F1 inverted-U, (3,3,3)=40.2%, peak (5,5,5)=43.4%, (9,9,9)=31.1%; default (6,6,6)=37.5%; top-K 3->10 improves 34.2%->39.5%
SVEN: VulInstruct F1 57.3%, recall 57.6%, precision 57.0%, accuracy 57.1%, P-C 24.7%, VP-S 14.1% (vs GPTLens 34.1% F1, Vul-RAG 26.7% F1)

Across models on PrimeVul: GPT-OSS-120B +VulInstruct F1 28.2->41.0 (+45.4%), recall +72.9%; Claude-Sonnet-4 VP-S 2.2->7.3 (+231.8%), P-C 3.2->6.9; DeepSeek-R1 best config P-C 22.8%, acc 56.6%, prec 62.8%

Cost: 150.6s and 18,433 input / 3,220 output tokens per case; retrieval 45.3s + scoring 55.3s dominate; optimized to 106.9s (inverted index) and 117.3s (parallel scoring); vs Vul-RAG 39,369 input tokens/115.1s and VulTrial 197.4s
LLM-as-Judge (GPT-5) calibration: 82% author agreement on 50 sampled cases (vs 92% for GPT-4o in original CORRECT)
Limitation analysis (DeepSeek-R1 +/- VulInstruct): +33 true positives, +11 false positives; FN->TP +16.7%, FP->TN -8.0%
baselines_compared: Chain-of-Thought / Ding et al. (DeepSeek-V3, prompting): F1 19.5%, recall 12.2%
ReVD (Qwen2.5-Coder, fine-tuned; 28K synthesized reasoning samples): F1 18.6%, recall 11.5%
VulTrial (GPT-4o fine-tuned, 4-agent): F1 30.9%, recall 20.8%, precision 59.9% (highest precision)
GPTLens (DeepSeek-V3, multi-agent auditor/critic): F1 33.9%, recall 25.0% (strongest baseline)
